perturbation.original_text: 6. Without your prior written consent, we will not for a period of one year from the date hereof directly or indirectly solicit for employment any person who is now employed by you or any of your subsidiaries who is identified by us as a result of our invastigation of the Business.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Without your prior written consent, we will not for a period of six months from the date hereof directly or indirectly solicit for employment any person who is now employed by you or any of your subsidiaries who is identified by us as a result of our invastigation of the Business.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to six months may conflict with what is considered a reasonable duration under Pennsylvania law, potentially making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania courts assess the reasonableness of non-compete/non-solicitation agreements based on several factors, including duration. While there's no fixed rule, six months may be deemed insufficient in some circumstances, particularly if the employee has access to sensitive information or customer relationships. The modification introduces uncertainty and a potential conflict with legal standards of reasonableness.
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the non-solicitation period from one year to six months. The Perturbation Explanation states that this reduced period 'may conflict with what is considered a reasonable duration under Pennsylvania law, potentially making the clause unenforceable.' The Law Explanation reinforces this by stating that courts assess the reasonableness of such agreements based on factors including duration, and six months 'may be deemed insufficient in some circumstances.' While the Law Snippets are missing, the explanations suggest that the reduced duration creates a potential conflict with legal standards of reasonableness under Pennsylvania law. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition matches this scenario, as the altered time-sensitive obligation (duration of non-solicitation) may not align with legal requirements for reasonableness.

perturbation.original_text: All copies of the Information, along with our notes on, summaries and compilations of, or excepts from, the Information, except for that portion of the Information which consists of analyses, compilations, forecasts, studies or other documents prepared by us, our agents, representatives or employees, will be returned to you immediately upon your request.
perturbation.changed_text: All copies of the Information, along with our notes on, summaries and compilations of, or excepts from, the Information, except for that portion of the Information which consists of analyses, compilations, forecasts, studies or other documents prepared by us, our agents, representatives or employees, will be returned to you within 60 days of your request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return timeframe to 'within 60 days' may violate Pennsylvania trade secret law, which implies a duty to promptly return confidential information upon request when the business relationship ends.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Pennsylvania Uniform Trade Secrets Act (PUTSA), while not explicitly mandating an immediate return, implies a duty to protect trade secrets. Delaying the return of confidential information for 60 days could be construed as a failure to take reasonable steps to protect those trade secrets, especially if the relationship has soured or there's a risk of misuse.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5304 (2022) - Damages :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5304 - Damages Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5304 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5304. Damages. (a) Monetary damages. --Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) Exemplary damages. --If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a 'within 60 days' timeframe for returning confidential information, which contradicts the implied duty under Pennsylvania trade secret law to promptly protect trade secrets, especially upon termination of a business relationship. The Law Explanation suggests that delaying the return could be viewed as a failure to reasonably protect trade secrets, particularly if there is risk of misuse. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 discusses damages for misappropriation (12 PA Cons Stat § 5304), the Law Explanation, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, suggests the changed text creates a legal contradiction because a 60-day delay could lead to potential misappropriation, as it fails to adequately protect those trade secrets. The modification makes it unclear whether the information is adequately protected, or returned in a commercially reasonable timeframe, creating uncertainty and potential non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 8. In the event that we or anyone to whom we transmit the Information pursuant to this Agreement becomes legally compelled to disclose any of the Information, we will provide you with prompt written notice and oral notice so that you may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. In the event that we or anyone to whom we transmit the Information pursuant to this Agreement becomes legally compelled to disclose any of the Information, we will provide you with notice within 10 days of the disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notice until after the disclosure violates the duty to provide 'prompt' notice, hindering the other party's ability to seek a protective order in a timely manner as guaranteed under Pennsylvania law related to court procedures and confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 4009.21
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania Rule of Civil Procedure 4009.21 deals with subpoenas. While not directly about confidentiality agreements, it reinforces the importance of timely notification when compelled disclosure is involved. Delaying notice for 10 days after disclosure significantly impairs the ability to seek a protective order, rendering the notification effectively useless.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 231 Pa. Code Rule 4009.21. Subpoena Upon a Person Not a Party for Production of Documents and Things. Prior Notice. Objections. SUBPOENA UPON A PERSON NOT A PARTY Rule 4009.21. Subpoena Upon a Person Not a Party for Production of Documents and Things. Prior Notice. Objections. (a) A party seeking production from a person not a party to the action shall give written notice to every other party of the intent to serve a subpoena at least twenty days before the date of service. A copy of the subpoena proposed to be served shall be attached to the notice. Official Note For the form of the written notice, see Rule 4009.24(a). These rules do not preclude (1) the issuance under Rule 234.1 et. seq. of a subpoena or request for the production of documents or things at a deposition pursuant to Rule 4007.1(d) or (2) an independent action against a person not a party for production of documents or things. (b) The written notice shall not be given to the person named in the subpoena. (c) Any party may object to the subpoena by filing of record written objections and serving a copy of the objections upon every other party to the action. Official Note For the form of the objections, see Rule 4009.24(b). The requirement of filing with the prothonotary the objections under this rule and the certificate under Rule 4009.23(a) provides a more formal procedure for the participation of a person not a party in the discovery process. (d)(1) If objections are received by the party intending to serve the subpoena prior to its service, the subpoena shall not be served. The court upon motion shall rule upon the objections and enter an appropriate order. Official Note Subdivision (a) of this rule provides a twenty-day notice period during which a subpoena may not be served. (2) If objections are not received as provided in paragraph (1), the subpoena may be served subject to the right of any party or interested person to seek a protective order. Official Note Rule 4009.22(a) requires the filing of a certificate as a pre
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the requirement for prompt notice. The original text specifies 'prompt written notice and oral notice' allowing the other party time to 'seek a protective order'. The changed text alters this by only requiring notice within 10 days of the disclosure. This delay undermines the ability to seek a protective order in a timely manner, which Pennsylvania Rule of Civil Procedure 4009.21 indirectly addresses by reinforcing the importance of timely notification. Although the snippet doesn't directly mention confidentiality agreements, the principle of timely notice to allow for objections or protective orders is evident in the procedure outlined in Rule 4009.21 regarding subpoenas. The delay introduced by the change effectively impairs the ability to act on the notification.

perturbation.original_text: 11. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Principality of Sealand.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing a jurisdiction with no established or recognized legal framework (Principality of Sealand) is against public policy and contradicts the principle of choosing a reasonable jurisdiction with connections to the contract, which would apply under Pennsylvania conflict of law principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Pennsylvania law permits parties to choose governing law, it implies that the chosen jurisdiction must have a reasonable relationship to the agreement and not violate public policy. Selecting the laws of a micronation like Sealand, which has no recognized legal system or connection to the parties, undermines the enforceability of the agreement and goes against established conflict of laws principles.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania as the governing law. The changed contract replaces this with the Principality of Sealand. The Law Explanation states this undermines the enforceability of the agreement because Sealand has no recognized legal system. The Perturbation Explanation states this contradicts choosing a reasonable jurisdiction with connections to the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes, implying a body of law that the contract should be governed by. Choosing Sealand, which has no such comparable legal framework, creates a contradiction because it renders the agreement's governing law provision unenforceable under typical conflict of laws principles.

perturbation.original_text: We acknowledge that you make no express or implied representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of the Information, and you expressly disclaim any and all liability that may be based on the Information, errors therein or omissions therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: We acknowledge that you make no express or implied representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of the Information, and you expressly disclaim any and all liability, including liability for intentional misrepresentation, that may be based on the Information, errors therein or omissions therefrom.
perturbation.explanation: Attempting to disclaim liability for intentional misrepresentation is against public policy and likely unenforceable under Pennsylvania law. You cannot contract out of liability for fraud.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law on fraud and misrepresentation
perturbation.law_explanation: While Pennsylvania law generally allows parties to disclaim warranties, disclaiming liability for intentional torts like fraud is against public policy. A party cannot contractually shield itself from the consequences of its own deliberate deceit. Such a disclaimer would be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to disclaim liability for intentional misrepresentation, which the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both state is against public policy and unenforceable under Pennsylvania law. While no specific statute is provided in the snippets, the legal principle against contracting out of liability for fraud is well-established. The changed text directly contradicts this principle, as it seeks to shield the party from consequences of intentional deceit.
